children.name: Sorex
children.description: Red-toothed shrews of the Holarctic — the most species-rich shrew genus, characterised by red-tipped teeth coated in iron-containing pigment that strengthens the tooth enamel. Shrews have one of the highest metabolic rates of any mammal: the common shrew must eat every 2–3 hours or die. Their hearts beat up to 1,320 times per minute and their metabolic rate is 50 times that of a human per unit body weight. In autumn, some species undergo Dehnel's phenomenon — the brain, skull, and internal organs actually shrink to reduce the energy cost of survival through winter, then regrow in spring.

children.name: Sorex araneus
children.description: Among the most abundant mammals in European woodland and hedgerow. Must consume 90% of its body weight daily. Undergoes dramatic Dehnel shrinkage in winter. Lives for just 14–19 months.

children.name: Sorex minutus
children.description: Weighs 3–6 g — one of the smallest land mammals in the world. Ireland's only native land insectivore. Must eat its own body weight every few hours. Astonishingly fierce for its size, taking invertebrates much larger than itself.

children.name: Crocidura
children.description: White-toothed shrews — the largest genus of any mammal with over 180 species. Unlike red-toothed shrews, their teeth lack iron pigment and remain white throughout life. White-toothed shrews are found across Africa, Europe, and Asia, and are among the most diverse mammals on Earth. Several island species show dramatic gigantism (Crocidura of the Canary Islands) or dwarfism compared to mainland relatives. Young white-toothed shrews form 'caravans' — the first infant grips its mother's tail, subsequent ones grip the preceding sibling, forming a chain that maintains family cohesion.

children.name: Crocidura russula
children.description: The commonest shrew in many parts of southern Europe. More tolerant of human habitation than red-toothed shrews. Young form characteristic caravans, each gripping the rump of the animal in front.

children.name: Crocidura suaveolens
children.description: Found across a vast range from western Europe to Japan. On Scilly and the Channel Islands it is the only shrew species. Island populations often show differences in body size from mainland forms.

children.name: Neomys
children.description: Water shrews — the largest shrews in Europe, specialised for aquatic hunting with fringes of stiff hairs on their hind feet and tail that act as paddles. They are venomous: saliva from submandibular glands contains blarina-toxin, a neurotoxin that immobilises earthworms, frogs, and small fish, allowing the shrew to store living but paralysed prey. Water shrews can dive to the bottom of streams and walk along the substrate hunting for invertebrates, fish eggs, and small fish. Their dense fur traps air bubbles that make them highly buoyant, so they must actively swim downward.

children.name: Neomys fodiens
children.description: Europe's largest shrew. Mildly venomous — a bite to a human causes localised pain and swelling. Swims with a distinctive bubble coating. Requires clean, fast-flowing streams with good invertebrate populations.

children.name: Suncus
children.description: Suncus shrews, including the Etruscan shrew — the world's smallest mammal by mass. The Etruscan shrew (Suncus etruscus) weighs just 1.8–2.5 grams — lighter than a 2p coin. Its heart beats 1,200 times per minute at rest and up to 1,511 times per minute during activity, the fastest heart rate of any known mammal. To fuel this metabolism it must eat almost continuously, consuming prey larger than itself. Despite its tiny size, it maintains a body temperature of 37°C and must eat every 30 minutes. The musk shrew (Suncus murinus) is a commensal species that has spread worldwide with human trade.

children.name: Suncus etruscus
children.description: The world's smallest mammal by mass at 1.8–2.5 g (kitti's hog-nosed bat is smaller by skull dimensions). Heart rate can exceed 1,500 bpm. Found in warm, dry scrubland around the Mediterranean.
